name: Ma Rainey
abstract: Gertrude "Ma" Rainey (née Pridgett; April 26, 1886 – December 22, 1939) was an American blues singer and influential early-blues recording artist. Dubbed the "Mother of the Blues", she bridged earlier vaudeville and the authentic expression of southern blues, influencing a generation of blues singers.

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Spottswood
about_quotes.evidence: I was listening to Ma Rainey singing “Don’t Fish in my Sea”... I’m working on a project for GHB Records... that is going to consist of all of the surviving Ma Rainey records
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Spottswood interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 13, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: I had learned that Ma Rainey, after she retired, in the last years of her life she bought a couple of theaters, including one in Rome, Georgia, and another in Columbus.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dianne Reeves
about_quotes.evidence: they played the music of the early 1900s, so it was Bessie Smith, Ma Rainey, all of these more blues oriented music. And so that was like always in the house.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dianne Reeves interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Clinton, New York, October 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Burton
about_quotes.evidence: I know a fellow here in Harlem that is a good saxophone player and a clarinet player that used to play with Ma Rainey

about_quotes.speaker: Nellie Lutcher
about_quotes.evidence: I don't know if I mentioned the fact that when I was a kid that Ma Rainey came through Lake Charles... we had her records... So we did realize that she was a very famous woman at that time.

about_quotes.speaker: Louis Armstrong: Jazz Casual
about_quotes.evidence: This one is called Countin' the Blues by Ma Rainey with the accompaniment by Louis Armstrong, Fletcher Henderson, group from the Fletcher Henderson Band.
